title: Credential Format Helper Guide
summary: Use this guide to understand credential format capacity, numbering headroom, facility-code/card-number limits, bit-length tradeoffs, migration pressure, and collision risk before badge populations grow into a format problem.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: Credential Format Helper estimates whether the selected credential format leaves enough numbering space for the expected badge population.
It helps catch format decisions that look fine for the first badge order but become painful later when more users, tenants, sites, contractors, replacements, or migration overlap are added.

The tool does not decode a proprietary card format. It provides planning pressure: how much of the available numbering space is being consumed and whether the selected format deserves review before rollout.

sections.title: When to use it
bullets: Before ordering a large badge or credential batch.
Before standardizing a credential format across a new site.
When deciding whether a legacy format still has enough life left.
When planning a multi-site, multi-building, or multi-tenant credential structure.
When a migration may require old and new credentials to coexist for a period of time.
When replacement cards, contractor badges, temporary badges, or inactive records could consume more range than the active employee count suggests.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These headings match the visible input labels on the calculator so the guide can be read directly against the form.

items.label: Facility Code Digits
items.description: The number of decimal digits allocated to the facility code portion of the credential. Facility codes are often used to separate sites, tenants, buildings, departments, or credential batches.
examples: 3 digits gives a planning range of about 000 through 999.
A single-site system may only use one facility code.
A multi-site or multi-tenant system may intentionally reserve different facility-code blocks.
items.whyItMatters: Facility-code headroom determines how cleanly the credential plan can be segmented. Without planned facility-code ranges, future sites or tenants may be forced into awkward numbering exceptions.
items.commonMistake: Assuming every visible facility-code value is valid for the reader, panel, credential format, and software platform without verifying the actual supported range.

links.label: Reader Type Selector guide
links.note: Use this related guide when format decisions also depend on reader interface, credential technology, or platform compatibility.

items.label: Card Number Digits
items.description: The number of decimal digits allocated to the individual card or badge number. This is usually the range consumed as unique credentials are issued.
examples: 5 digits gives a planning range of about 00000 through 99999.
A site with 500 users may still need far more than 500 available numbers after spares, replacements, contractors, and inactive records are considered.
items.whyItMatters: Card-number capacity is usually the most direct limit on how many unique credentials can be issued before overlap, collision, or migration pressure appears.
items.commonMistake: Sizing the range only for current employees and ignoring lifecycle consumption from lost cards, replacement badges, temporary users, and future growth.

items.label: Format Type
items.description: The method used for planning capacity. Decimal uses facility-code digits multiplied by card-number digits. Binary estimates capacity from selected bit length after a simple parity allowance.
examples: Decimal is useful when the site manages credentials by printed facility code and printed card number.
Binary is useful when comparing common bit lengths such as 26-bit, 34-bit, 37-bit, or 48-bit.
items.whyItMatters: The same badge population can look safe or risky depending on the real constraint: printed numbering, bit length, reader support, panel support, or manufacturer-specific format structure.
items.commonMistake: Treating a bit length as the whole format. A 37-bit format, for example, still needs to be understood in terms of fields, facility code behavior, card number range, and platform compatibility.

items.label: Bit Length (if Binary)
items.description: The credential bit length used for approximate binary capacity planning. The calculator applies a simple usable-bit estimate and does not decode proprietary card structures.
examples: 26-bit is a common legacy planning reference.
34-bit, 37-bit, and 48-bit formats usually provide more numbering room, depending on how the fields are structured.
items.whyItMatters: Bit length affects theoretical capacity, but real-world support still depends on readers, panels, card programming, software settings, and site standards.
items.commonMistake: Assuming a larger bit length automatically works everywhere in an existing system.

items.label: Estimated Badge Population
items.description: The expected number of unique credentials that must fit inside the selected format. This should include more than current active employees.
examples: Include staff, contractors, temporary badges, spare cards, future hires, replacements, inactive records, and migration overlap.
For multi-tenant systems, each tenant may need reserved numbering space instead of sharing one flat pool.
items.whyItMatters: Badge population drives the capacity-used percentage and determines whether the format has comfortable headroom or is approaching collision risk.
items.commonMistake: Using only today’s employee count and ignoring the actual lifecycle of credentials.

links.label: Access Level Sizing guide
links.note: Use this related guide when credential population also affects roles, schedules, door groups, and permission complexity.

sections.title: How to read Capacity Used
body: Capacity Used is the percentage of the available credential numbering space consumed by the estimated badge population.

A low percentage means the format has room for growth, replacements, temporary credentials, and migration overlap. A high percentage means the site is getting closer to collision, partitioning, or migration pressure.
This number should not be read as an exact manufacturer limit. It is a planning pressure indicator that helps decide whether the selected format is comfortable, tight, or risky.
bullets: Low utilization: the format likely has comfortable numbering headroom.
Moderate utilization: the format may work, but growth and credential lifecycle should be reviewed.
High utilization: format strategy should be reviewed before large rollout.
Near or over capacity: choose a larger or more carefully partitioned format before deployment.

sections.title: Healthy / Watch / Risk interpretation
body: The status is based on how much of the selected credential space is consumed by the estimated badge population.

HEALTHY means the format has enough headroom for normal growth assumptions. WATCH means the format is still possible but should be reviewed. RISK means the format is likely too constrained for the population or lifecycle assumptions.
bullets: HEALTHY: document the format and keep the numbering plan consistent.
WATCH: review growth, tenant separation, replacement cards, and migration overlap before rollout.
RISK: consider a larger format, better partitioning, or a different credential strategy.

sections.title: Real-world planning workflow
bullets: Start with the expected badge population, then add realistic lifecycle headroom.
Decide whether the system needs one flat numbering pool or separated facility-code blocks.
Confirm reader and controller compatibility before committing to bit length or format type.
Document the chosen format, facility-code range, card-number range, and any reserved blocks.
If migrating, plan how old and new credentials will coexist during the transition.
Use the final result as a design-review checkpoint before ordering credentials.

sections.title: Format migration notes
body: Credential format changes can be disruptive because they touch cards, readers, panels, software settings, user records, and enrollment workflows.
If the current format is tight, it may be better to migrate before the system grows larger, rather than waiting until numbering collisions or range exhaustion force the issue.
bullets: Check whether the platform can support multiple formats during a transition.
Confirm whether existing readers can read the proposed credential type.
Plan whether old credentials will remain active, be reissued, or be translated.
Reserve enough numbering space for both old and new populations during the overlap period.

links.label: Reader Type Selector guide
links.note: Use this guide when migration depends on reader technology, credential type, or interface compatibility.

sections.title: Red flags
bullets: Capacity Used is high before future growth is included.
The site uses multiple undocumented facility codes.
Different buildings or tenants are sharing ranges without a clear partitioning plan.
The reader population includes mixed technologies with unclear format support.
The planned credential batch leaves little room for lost-card replacement or temporary badges.
No one can explain the difference between the printed number and the decoded controller value.

sections.title: Documentation checklist
bullets: Credential format name or standard.
Format Type used for planning.
Bit Length, if applicable.
Facility Code Digits and intended facility-code ranges.
Card Number Digits and intended card-number ranges.
Estimated Badge Population including growth and lifecycle assumptions.
Reader and panel compatibility assumptions.
Migration or coexistence plan, if replacing an existing format.

sections.title: Common mistakes
bullets: Counting only current employees instead of total credential lifecycle demand.
Forgetting spare credentials, replacement cards, contractors, temporary badges, and inactive records.
Mixing credential formats without documenting how the platform interprets each one.
Assuming printed numbers and controller-decoded numbers always match.
Choosing a format based only on today’s site count instead of the expected service life of the system.
Assuming a larger bit length automatically works with all existing readers and panels.

sections.title: Assumptions and limits
bullets: Decimal capacity is planning math based on facility-code digits multiplied by card-number digits.
Binary capacity is approximate and uses a basic parity allowance.
This tool does not decode proprietary manufacturer credential structures.
This tool does not verify reader, panel, software, or card-vendor compatibility.
Final credential decisions should be verified against the access-control platform, reader compatibility, card vendor, and site operating policy.
